assumptions: The question concerns hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements under IFRS 9.
The intragroup dividend has been declared or otherwise recognised so that a corresponding receivable/payable exists.
The relevant group entities may have different functional currencies.
Absent contrary facts, the intragroup dividend itself is assumed not to affect consolidated profit or loss.
The intended designation is limited to the foreign currency risk component.

approaches.label: General intragroup prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally be exposures with parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup items do not qualify unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: A dividend cannot be hedged in consolidation merely because it exists within the group.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 sets the baseline rule that only external-party items qualify in consolidation.

approaches.label: Recognized intragroup monetary item FX hedge
approaches.reasoning: A recognised intragroup receivable/payable may qualify in consolidation if it is a monetary item whose foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated. The FX risk component can be designated only if it is reliably measurable and the hedge is properly documented.
conditions: The recognised dividend balance is a monetary intragroup item
The counterparties have different functional currencies
Foreign exchange gains or losses on the item are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedged FX component is reliably measurable
IFRS 9 formal designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: Once the receivable exists, hedge documentation may be possible in consolidation if the item creates residual consolidated FX exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements.
Paragraphs 6.3.2, 6.3.7 and 6.4.1 require reliable measurement plus formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction hedge
approaches.reasoning: A forecast intragroup transaction qualifies in consolidation only if its FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. On the assumed facts, an intragroup dividend does not meet that profit-or-loss condition.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not base consolidated hedge documentation on the dividend before the receivable/payable creates a qualifying monetary exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions only when FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph B6.3.5 explains that intragroup items such as royalties, interest or management charges often fail because they do not affect consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge of foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 is limited to hedges of the foreign currency risk arising from a net investment in a foreign operation, measured by reference to net assets. A dividend receivable is not the same hedged item as that qualifying net investment.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not frame the dividend receivable hedge as a net investment hedge unless the actual hedged item is the qualifying net investment itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7, 8, 10, 11 and 12 confine the model to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and to qualifying net asset amounts.
Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a distinct hedge accounting category.
